page: Vultr vx1-g-96c-384g-5760s is a high-capacity instance within the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD processor with 96 vCPUs and 96 physical cores. The system operates with a single thread per core and utilizes a shared CPU allocation model. It is equipped with 384.0 GB of system memory, providing a balanced ratio of 4.0 GB of RAM per core.

For local storage, the instance includes a bundled 5760 GB NVMe SSD, offering high-capacity local storage directly attached to the instance. It does not include any dedicated GPU hardware. Network connectivity is supported by one complimentary public IPv4 address.

The primary architectural tradeoff of this instance is its shared CPU allocation, which may introduce resource contention under sustained multi-core workloads compared to dedicated CPU instances. However, this shared model enhances qualitative cost efficiency by offering a high density of vCPUs, memory, and NVMe storage at a lower resource-tier cost.

This server is suited for large-scale general-purpose workloads, data processing, distributed databases, and staging environments that require substantial memory and local NVMe storage capacity but can tolerate the performance variability associated with shared CPU resources.
description: The Vultr vx1-g-96c-384g-5760s is a high-capacity instance in the VX1 Cloud Compute family, featuring an AMD x86_64 processor with 96 vCPUs and 96 physical cores operating at one thread per core. It is configured with 384.0 GB of system memory, establishing a balanced 4.0 GB per core ratio. Storage is supported by a bundled 5760 GB NVMe SSD, while GPU acceleration is absent. A key architectural characteristic is its shared CPU allocation, which presents a tradeoff of potential resource contention but offers high qualitative cost efficiency for resource-dense deployments. This server is suited for large-scale general-purpose applications, database hosting, and data processing tasks that benefit from extensive local storage and memory capacity without requiring dedicated CPU guarantees.
